_meta.note: Live data store for FlyScout. Entries with auto_detected:false are real, sourced, researched historical entries. Entries with auto_detected:true are written exclusively by check_rules.py via GitHub Actions — do not hand-edit those.

changes.title: Mastercard Excessive Authorization Integrity Fee raised to $0.30
changes.summary: Mastercard's Excessive Authorization Integrity Fee increased from $0.15 to $0.30 on Jan 1 2024 — the second step in a three-year escalation schedule ($0.10 in 2022, $0.15 in Oct 2023, $0.30 in Jan 2024, $0.50 in Jan 2025).
changes.source_name: Mastercard Interchange Rates — Merchant Cost Consulting
changes.new_snippet: Mastercard increased its excessive authorization integrity fee to $0.30 on January 1, 2024 and $0.50 on January 1, 2025.

changes.title: Mastercard Excessive Authorization Integrity Fee raised to $0.50
changes.summary: Third consecutive annual increase to Mastercard's Excessive Authorization Integrity Fee — now $0.50, up from $0.30. The fee has risen 5x since 2022 ($0.10 → $0.50 over three years), targeting excessive account-testing authorization attempts.
changes.source_name: Mastercard Interchange Rates — Merchant Cost Consulting
changes.new_snippet: $0.50 on January 1, 2025.

changes.title: Visa Digital Commerce Service Fee expanded to all CNP authorization attempts
changes.summary: Visa's CNP-specific Digital Commerce Service Fee (0.0075%) originally applied only to cleared/settled transactions. As of Jan 2025 it applies to ALL approved or denied CNP authorization attempts — a significant scope expansion with direct impact on card-not-present cost structures.
changes.old_value: 0.0075% applied to cleared/settled CNP transactions only
changes.new_value: 0.0075% applied to all approved or denied CNP transactions
changes.source_name: Visa Interchange Rates — Merchant Cost Consulting
changes.old_snippet: Visa Digital Commerce Service Fee on card-not-present transactions: 0.0075%, applied to cleared and settled transactions.
changes.new_snippet: As of January 1, 2025, the Digital Commerce Service Fee will apply to all approved or denied card-not-present transactions (not just cleared and settled).

changes.title: Visa/Mastercard antitrust settlement caps US consumer credit interchange at 125bps
changes.summary: Court-supervised 2024 Settlement: Visa and Mastercard agreed to cap US consumer credit interchange for Visa Traditional/Trad. Rewards and Mastercard Core/Enhanced Value at 125bps for 8 years, with a further 10bps audited reduction. Average domestic credit interchange also reduced by 4-7bps. Surcharge rules updated: cap is 3% if applied uniformly across all card brands, or 1% if applied selectively to Visa/Mastercard only.
changes.old_value: No interchange cap; 4% surcharge cap (uniform required)
changes.new_value: 125bps cap on qualifying consumer credit; 3% surcharge cap (uniform) or 1% (selective)
changes.source_name: Visa/Mastercard Interchange Settlement — Davis Wright Tremaine
changes.new_snippet: Visa and Mastercard will cap interchange at 125-bps for qualifying consumer credit cards for 8 years. Surcharge cap is 3% if applied uniformly, 1% if applied selectively.

changes.title: Fed Reserve Regulation II debit cap proposal — comment period extended to May 2024
changes.summary: The Federal Reserve's Oct 2023 proposal to cut the Regulation II debit interchange base from 21¢ to 14.4¢ (plus ad valorem 5bps→4bps, fraud adjustment 1¢→1.3¢) had its public comment period extended from Feb 12 to May 12, 2024. Represents an open regulatory process directly affecting US debit interchange.
changes.old_value: Proposed: base 21¢ + 5bps + 1¢ fraud adjustment; comment period ending Feb 12 2024
changes.source_name: Federal Register — Debit Card Interchange Fees Extension of Comment Period
changes.new_snippet: The Board is extending the comment period for 90 days, until May 12, 2024.

changes.title: Court vacates Regulation II debit interchange cap (stayed pending appeal)
changes.summary: US District Court (North Dakota) in Corner Post v. Federal Reserve vacated Regulation II entirely, ruling the Fed exceeded statutory authority under the Durbin Amendment. The vacatur was stayed pending appeal to the Eighth Circuit — meaning the existing 21¢ + 5bps cap remains legally in force for now, but the legal status of the cap is now under serious challenge.
changes.old_value: Regulation II cap: 21¢ + 5bps + 1¢ fraud adj (legally settled)
changes.new_value: Vacated by court order — stayed pending Eighth Circuit appeal (cap remains in effect)
changes.source_name: District Court Vacates Regulation II — Cooley
changes.new_snippet: The Court vacated Regulation II and stayed the vacatur pending appeal. The Federal Reserve exceeded its statutory authority in setting the debit interchange cap.

changes.title: UK-EEA cross-border CNP interchange confirmed at 1.15% debit / 1.5% credit
changes.summary: Post-Brexit, the EU Interchange Fee Regulation cap (0.2% debit / 0.3% credit) no longer applies to UK-EEA cross-border transactions. Visa and Mastercard raised cross-border card-not-present interchange to 1.15% debit and 1.5% credit — roughly 5x the prior capped rate — with direct cost impact for any merchant serving UK and EEA customers online.
changes.source_name: The Future of Interchange Fees: 2026 — Clearly Payments
changes.new_snippet: After Brexit, cross-border online transaction fees rose to 1.15% for debit and 1.5% for credit between UK merchants and EEA-issued cards.

changes.title: UK PSR publishes market review into card-acquiring services
changes.summary: The Payment Systems Regulator launched a market review into card-acquiring services, scrutinising interchange and merchant pricing structures to assess whether they deliver good outcomes for merchants and cardholders. Findings fed into ongoing pricing discussions between PSR and the major networks and may lead to further regulatory action.
changes.source_name: UK Payment Systems Regulator — Card Acquiring Market Review
changes.new_snippet: The PSR is reviewing the card-acquiring market to assess whether competition is working well for merchants and cardholders.

changes.title: EU IFR domestic caps stable — 0.2% debit / 0.3% credit
changes.summary: EU Regulation 2015/751 domestic interchange caps (0.2% debit / 0.3% credit) remain unchanged and in force. No modification to EU domestic rates occurred during the Jan 2024 – Jun 2026 monitoring window — included as a confirmed baseline for the dashboard and prediction engine.
changes.source_name: EU Interchange Fee Regulation (EU) 2015/751
changes.new_snippet: Interchange fees for consumer debit cards shall not exceed 0.2% and for consumer credit cards shall not exceed 0.3% of the value of the transaction.

changes.title: Interregional interchange caps (non-EEA cards) extended to November 2029
changes.summary: The European Commission confirmed Visa and Mastercard voluntarily extended caps on interregional interchange (non-EEA-issued cards used within EEA) through November 2029. Rates: 0.2%/0.3% for in-person, 1.15%/1.5% for online (CNP). This is highly relevant for any EEA merchant accepting foreign-issued cards online — the CNP cap of 1.5% credit would otherwise revert to uncapped network rates.
changes.new_value: Extended to November 2029: 0.20% debit / 0.30% credit (physical); 1.15% debit / 1.50% credit (CNP)
changes.source_name: Visa and Mastercard extend fee caps on non-EU cards until 2029 — FStech
changes.old_snippet: Caps on interregional interchange fees due to expire November 2024.
changes.new_snippet: Visa and Mastercard voluntarily extended caps until November 2029. Online: 1.15% debit / 1.50% credit. In-person: 0.20% debit / 0.30% credit.

changes.title: RBA opens Review of Merchant Card Payment Costs and Surcharging
changes.summary: The Reserve Bank of Australia published an Issues Paper launching a formal review of card payment costs and surcharging — the first step in a process that would lead to the most significant overhaul of Australian card payment rules in over a decade.
changes.source_name: RBA Review of Merchant Card Payment Costs and Surcharging — Issues Paper
changes.new_snippet: The RBA published an Issues Paper in October 2024 to begin Phase 1 of the Review, seeking stakeholder views on merchant card payment costs and surcharging.

changes.title: RBA Consultation Paper: preliminary view to ban surcharging, cut interchange
changes.summary: Phase 2 of the RBA review: Payments System Board's preliminary view is to ban surcharging entirely on eftpos, Mastercard, and Visa designated networks, reduce the domestic credit interchange cap to 0.3%, and increase transparency requirements. Industry feedback period closed August 26, 2025.
changes.new_value: Proposed: surcharging banned on eftpos/Mastercard/Visa; domestic credit cap reduced to 0.3%
changes.source_name: RBA Media Release — Consultation Paper on Card Payments
changes.new_snippet: The PSB has reached the preliminary view that it would be in the public interest to remove surcharging on eftpos, Mastercard and Visa cards and to reduce interchange fees.

changes.title: RBA extends review timeline to March 2026 after 174 submissions
changes.summary: The RBA pushed back the conclusion of its surcharging and interchange review from late 2025 to March 2026, citing the volume and substance of 174 written submissions requiring consideration.
changes.source_name: No surcharging clarity until 2026 — SmartCompany
changes.new_snippet: The RBA confirmed its investigation will carry through to 2026 to consider the 174 submissions received.

changes.title: RBA Final Decision: surcharging banned from Oct 2026, interchange caps cut
changes.summary: Final RBA Conclusions Paper: surcharging removed entirely on debit, prepaid, and credit for designated networks (eftpos, Mastercard, Visa); domestic interchange caps reduced; foreign-card interchange caps added. Most changes effective 1 October 2026. Foreign-card caps and remaining transparency requirements effective 1 April 2027. This concludes the consultation process begun in October 2024.
changes.new_value: Surcharging banned from 1 Oct 2026; domestic interchange caps cut; foreign-card cap from 1 Apr 2027
changes.source_name: RBA Conclusions Paper — Merchant Card Payment Costs and Surcharging
changes.old_snippet: Surcharging permitted on the basis of cost of acceptance.
changes.new_snippet: A package of reforms including removing surcharging, reducing interchange fees and increasing transparency will come into effect on 1 October 2026.

changes.title: Credit card surcharging legalized in Canada — 2.4% cap
changes.summary: Following a class-action settlement, Visa and Mastercard permitted Canadian merchants (outside Quebec) to surcharge credit card transactions, capped at 2.4% or the merchant's effective discount rate, whichever is lower. Applies to both card-present and CNP. Debit and prepaid excluded. 30 days advance notice to acquirer required. Quebec prohibited by provincial Consumer Protection Act.
changes.new_value: Surcharging permitted on credit cards, capped at 2.4% (ex-Quebec; CNP included)
changes.source_name: Mastercard Canada — Merchant Surcharge Rules
changes.new_snippet: The Maximum Surcharge Cap: 2.4%. Merchants can apply a brand-level or product-level surcharge to Mastercard credit cards, capped at the lesser of the merchant's effective discount rate or 2.4%.

changes.title: Visa Canada simplifies surcharge notification — acquirer-only notice required
changes.summary: Visa updated its Canadian surcharge rules: from April 15 2023, merchants only need to notify their acquirer 30 days in advance of surcharging, removing the previous requirement to also notify Visa directly. Also clarified that the surcharge amount must be populated in Field 28 (Amount, Transaction Fee) in authorization messages.
changes.old_value: 30 days notice required to both Visa AND acquirer
changes.new_value: 30 days notice to acquirer only (Visa notification no longer required)
changes.source_name: Updates to Visa Surcharge Rules in Canada — Nuvei
changes.old_snippet: Merchants must notify both Visa and their acquirer at least 30 days in advance.
changes.new_snippet: From April 15, 2023, merchants will only be required to notify their acquirers, not Visa, 30 days in advance of commencing surcharging.

changes.title: Small business interchange relief — ~0.95% average effective credit rate
changes.summary: As part of the class-action settlement, Visa and Mastercard agreed to reduced interchange rates for eligible small businesses in Canada, targeting approximately 0.95% average effective credit interchange — a meaningful reduction for qualifying small merchants compared to standard rates.
changes.old_value: Standard rates (average ~1.4% for Canadian credit cards)
changes.new_value: ~0.95% average effective credit interchange (eligible small merchants)
changes.source_name: The Future of Interchange Fees: 2026 — Clearly Payments
changes.old_snippet: Average credit interchange in Canada is approximately 1.4%.
changes.new_snippet: Small-business relief targets ~0.95% average effective credit interchange for eligible merchants.

changes.title: MAS confirms no interchange caps in Singapore; parliamentary fee scrutiny
changes.summary: Singapore has no government-mandated interchange caps — rates are set solely by the networks. Surcharging is permitted without major restriction. In March 2026 MAS responded to written parliamentary questions on whether it tracks interchange fee revenue and monitors merchant costs from Visa/Mastercard fees. This represents active oversight but not yet a concrete rule change — honest thin coverage reflecting actual available evidence.
changes.source_name: MAS Written Reply to Parliamentary Questions on Visa and Mastercard Fees
changes.new_snippet: MAS fielded parliamentary questions on whether it tracks Visa and Mastercard interchange fee revenue and merchant costs — March 2026.

changes.title: JFTC/METI require Visa and Mastercard to disclose interchange fees
changes.summary: Japan's Fair Trade Commission and METI pressed Visa and Mastercard to disclose interchange fees to ensure fair competitive conditions. Part of Japan's broader cashless policy push — Japan reached 42.8% cashless ratio in 2024 (per METI March 2025), exceeding its 40% target. No specific Visa/Mastercard rate change was found for Japan since Jan 2024 despite a dedicated search; this baseline entry captures the transparency framework and confirmed context.
changes.source_name: Payments Regulation in Asia — CMSPI
changes.new_snippet: Japan's METI required card networks to disclose interchange fees in September 2022 to ensure fair competitive conditions.
